name: Sohrab
description: Sohrab is a tragic hero from Persian mythology, most famously known from Ferdowsi's epic poem, the Shahnameh (Book of Kings). He is the son of Rostam, the greatest warrior in Persian legend, and Tahmineh, the princess of Samangan. Unbeknownst to Rostam, Tahmineh conceals her pregnancy and raises Sohrab in secret, fearing that Rostam's enemies would harm the child. Sohrab grows into a powerful and skilled warrior, rivaling even his father in strength and prowess. Driven by a desire to find his father and prove his worth, Sohrab sets out to conquer Iran, believing that Rostam will join him once he sees his son's might. He leads the Turanian army against the Iranian forces, engaging in numerous battles and demonstrating his unmatched martial abilities. The tragic climax of Sohrab's story arrives when he faces Rostam in single combat. Neither man knows the other's true identity, and their battle is fierce and prolonged. Rostam, using a trick he learned from his father, mortally wounds Sohrab. As Sohrab lies dying, he reveals his identity, and Rostam is overcome with grief and remorse at having killed his own son. Sohrab's story is a poignant tale of missed connections, fate, and the devastating consequences of war and ignorance. He represents youthful strength, unrealized potential, and the tragedy of familial conflict.
subtitle: Tragic hero of the Shahnameh
achievements: Mastered combat skills at a young age
Led the Turanian army against Iran
Proved his strength and valor in battle

keyMyths.title: Birth and Secret Upbringing
keyMyths.description: Sohrab was born in secret to Rostam and Tahmineh. Tahmineh hid his existence from Rostam, raising him in Samangan and concealing his true parentage to protect him.

keyMyths.title: Sohrab's Quest
keyMyths.description: Driven by ambition and a desire to find his father, Sohrab sets out to conquer Iran. He believes that Rostam will recognize him and join his cause once he sees his power.

keyMyths.title: The Fatal Duel
keyMyths.description: Sohrab and Rostam engage in a fierce duel, unaware of their familial connection. Rostam ultimately defeats and kills Sohrab, only to discover his identity after it is too late.

sources.author: Ferdowsi
sources.title: Shahnameh (The Book of Kings)
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Sohrab and Rostam, detailing their tragic encounter.

sources.author: Dick Davis
sources.title: Stories from the Shahnameh
sources.description: A modern English translation and retelling of key episodes from the Shahnameh, including the Sohrab and Rostam narrative.

associations.name: Sword
associations.significance: Represents Sohrab's martial prowess and his destiny as a warrior.

associations.name: Samangan
associations.significance: Sohrab's birthplace and the location where he was raised in isolation from his father.

associations.name: Father-Son Conflict
associations.significance: Symbolizes the tragic consequences of war and the loss of familial connection.

associations.name: Armband
associations.significance: The armband given by Rostam to Tahmineh, meant to identify their son, but ultimately fails to prevent tragedy.

associations.name: Strength
associations.significance: Sohrab's immense physical power and fighting ability, inherited from his father.

associations.name: Tragedy
associations.significance: The overarching theme of Sohrab's life, marked by unintended consequences and fatal misunderstandings.

associations.name: Tahmineh
associations.significance: Represents maternal love and sacrifice, as well as the burden of concealing Sohrab's identity.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are legendary warriors with a fatal flaw and a premature death in battle.
Both are figures of great promise whose deaths bring about immense grief and signify a tragic loss.
Both are exceptionally skilled warriors who face tragic fates, including battling and killing someone they were closely related to, unknowingly.

cultural.modernLegacy: Sohrab's story continues to resonate in Persian literature, art, and theater, serving as a powerful reminder of the human cost of war and the importance of family. His tale is frequently adapted and reinterpreted in contemporary works, exploring themes of identity, destiny, and the consequences of misunderstanding. He remains a symbol of tragic heroism and a testament to the enduring power of the Shahnameh.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Concealment
extendedContent.content: Sohrab's early life is shrouded in secrecy. Born to Rostam and Tahmineh in Samangan, his existence is hidden from Rostam to protect him from the enemies of his father. Tahmineh raises Sohrab alone, instilling in him a sense of his lineage and potential greatness. From a young age, Sohrab displays extraordinary strength and martial aptitude, quickly surpassing his peers in combat skills. He yearns to emulate his father's legendary feats, unaware of Rostam's identity. Stories of Rostam's valor fuel Sohrab's ambition, driving him to seek out his father and prove his own worth. This ambition, combined with his naivete and lack of paternal guidance, sets the stage for the tragic events that follow. He becomes a formidable warrior, eager to test his skills and establish his own reputation, all while unknowingly heading towards a devastating confrontation.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and the Fatal Duel
extendedContent.content: Sohrab's most significant quest is his attempt to conquer Persia and install himself as its ruler, believing he can then unite with his father, Rostam. He leads an army towards Persia, his path inevitably leading him to face the Persian forces. The climax of Sohrab's story arrives when he encounters Rostam on the battlefield. Neither recognizes the other, and they engage in a fierce duel. Despite Sohrab's impressive strength and skill, Rostam, with his greater experience, gains the upper hand. In a moment of desperation, Rostam fatally wounds Sohrab. As Sohrab lies dying, he reveals a sign given to him by his mother – an armband proving his parentage. Rostam is overcome with grief and remorse upon realizing he has killed his own son. The battle ends with Rostam lamenting the loss of his son, and the armies are left in shock and despair.

extendedContent.title: Legacy of a Tragic Hero
extendedContent.content: Sohrab's legacy is one of tragic potential and the devastating consequences of war and misunderstanding. He embodies the qualities of a great hero – strength, courage, and ambition – but his lack of knowledge and the circumstances of his birth lead to his downfall. The story of Sohrab serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of communication, the destructive nature of conflict, and the enduring power of familial bonds. His death is a profound loss, not only for Rostam but also for the potential future he represented. Sohrab's story continues to be retold and reinterpreted, ensuring that his memory lives on as a symbol of tragic heroism and a reminder of the human cost of war. He remains a compelling figure in Persian literature and a poignant example of the complexities of fate and identity.

shortDescription: A tragic hero in Persian mythology, Sohrab is Rostam's son who unknowingly battles and is killed by his father, highlighting themes of fate, war, and familial bonds.
